meta.title: Motor Power Calculation (Torque, Speed, Efficiency Explained)
meta.description: Learn how to calculate required motor power using torque, rotational speed, and efficiency. Step-by-step sizing guide for mechanical systems.

hero.h1: Motor Power Calculation (Torque, Speed, Efficiency Explained)
hero.intro: Motor power calculation is essential in mechanical and electrical systems. It determines the required energy to drive loads like conveyors, pumps, and fans. Proper calculation ensures you don't oversize (wasting money) or undersize (causing motor failure).

variables.ω: Rotational speed in radians per second (rad/s)

stepByStep: Measure or calculate the required load torque (T).
Determine the required rotational speed in RPM.
Determine the mechanical efficiency factor of the transmission (η).
Apply the formula to calculate power in Watts.
whyThisMatters: Crucial for proper motor sizing to prevent stalling under heavy loads.
Ensures energy savings by operating the motor close to its peak efficiency curve.
Prevents thermal overload and catastrophic motor failure.
commonMistakes: Ignoring transmission efficiency (gearboxes and belts consume power).
Wrong unit conversion, particularly confusing RPM with rad/s.
Underestimating startup load or peak dynamic forces.
engineeringTip: Always apply a safety factor (usually 1.15 to 1.25) to your calculated power to account for unexpected overloads and voltage fluctuations.

calculatorCta.label: Use the Motor Power Calculator to size your system correctly

faq.question: What is the relationship between torque and power?
faq.answer: Power is the rate at which work is done. Torque is the rotational force. Power equals torque multiplied by the rotational speed.

faq.question: How do I convert kW to Horsepower (HP)?
faq.answer: 1 kW is approximately equal to 1.34 HP. Conversely, 1 HP equals 0.746 kW.

faq.question: What causes motor efficiency to drop?
faq.answer: Running a motor at less than 50% of its rated load significantly reduces its efficiency. Heat, voltage imbalance, and worn bearings also degrade efficiency.
